## Why standard regression fails with text

The document-term matrix typically has far more columns than rows ($n \ll p$).
Standard OLS cannot estimate more parameters than observations, so ordinary linear
regression on raw text features will fail or overfit catastrophically.

In `GRA 4164`, this is the entry point for penalised regression. Lasso adds a
penalty $\lambda \sum_j |\beta_j|$ that forces most coefficients to zero, keeping
only the most predictive terms. Ridge regression shrinks all coefficients without
zeroing them out. Cross-validation selects the penalty strength $\lambda$ that
generalises best to held-out data.

The key insight for text applications is that most terms in the vocabulary are
irrelevant to any given prediction task. Penalisation exploits this sparsity.

## Naive Bayes classification

When the outcome is categorical — spam or ham, positive or negative, topic A or
topic B — naive Bayes is a natural starting point. Despite its simplicity, it works
remarkably well for document classification.

The classifier applies Bayes' rule to compute the probability that a document
belongs to each class:

$$
v_{k,\text{NB}} = \arg\max_{v_k} \; P(v_k) \prod_{j} P(c_j \mid v_k)
$$

The "naive" assumption is that word occurrences are conditionally independent given
the class. This is obviously wrong for natural language — words are not independent —
but the assumption simplifies computation to mere counting and still produces
competitive classifiers.

**Estimation** reduces to counting: the prior $\hat{P}(v_k) = n_k / n$ is the class
proportion, and the likelihood uses Laplace-smoothed frequencies:

$$
\hat{P}(c_j \mid v_k) = \frac{\text{count}(c_j, v_k) + 1}{\sum \text{count}(c, v_k) + p}
$$

The $+1$ in the numerator prevents zero probabilities from collapsing the entire
product.

## When to use which

Text regressions are the natural choice when the outcome is continuous (GDP growth,
stock returns, review ratings). Naive Bayes is preferred for categorical outcomes
(spam detection, sentiment polarity, document routing). Both methods take the
document-term matrix as input and both benefit from the same preprocessing pipeline.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students why the "naive" independence assumption works well in practice despite
being wrong. The key insight is that classification only requires getting the ranking
of class probabilities right, not their exact values — and independent-feature models
are surprisingly good at ranking.
:::

## What to report

When students present a text regression or classification in this course, they
should report:

- the target variable and whether it is continuous or categorical,
- the text features used (raw counts, TF-IDF, or other weighting),
- the regularisation method and how $\lambda$ was chosen (for regression),
- at least one evaluation metric appropriate to the task (MSE for regression;
  accuracy, precision, and recall for classification),
- and a short discussion of whether the model captures a meaningful relationship
  or is driven by spurious term associations.
